text_clean: El museo de chan chan es un lugar accesorio a las ruinas de chan chan, donde la entrada viene incluida con la ciudad de barro. No hay que contratar guía aunque ellos te lo recomienden (para sacar dinero). Dentro del museo encontrarás distintos objetos y arte de esta cultura. Tienen una sala con una proyección y una maqueta gigante en el suelo en modo interactivo que debes pedir que enciendan (ya que si no lo pides no lo ponen), en ella se observa como fue la evolución de la ciudad y el alcance de su expansión (merece la pena, de verdad). Tienen perros calatos (peruanos) en la entrada y suelen entrar por el museo. Los del museo se negaban a poner la protección para ahorrarse el dinero y el trabajo, por eso mi valoración tan baja. Hay que exigirles que lo pongan para verlo. No dejes de aprovechar tu entrada en la ciudad para ver este museo.

text_clean: This was a site visited as part of our large tour group that was in town for a few days en route to Machu Picchu. Saqsaywaman was interesting and it is very impressive in terms of the size of the stones and the precision of the placement but I must admit to being somewhere underwhelmed after that, there isn't much else to do here.... aside from visit the large Jesus statue on the way up the hill. If you have the chance to choose between this and touring the beautiful palace in Cusco, you may want to choose the palace.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: The content of the museum is amazing and some of the best I've seen in South America. Unfortunately the museum is massively let down by the lack of story and content conveyed by the information boards. Also none of the information on the second and third, the lowest level, has any translation into other languages and the top level only into English (about 25% of the whole museum) so its pointless going if you want to learn and not just see pretty objects. I believe they have the option of a guide to squeeze more money from you if you like. I'd recommend reading up beforehand so you know what your going to see.

text_clean: If you choose to take the 1-day-ticket it includes four inka places that are located along the 28G-road north of Cusco. Sacsayhuaman is the main one. Take a bus from the main road to go to the attraction on top first (Tambomachay). From there walk down the road to see the other ones and finish with Sacsayhuaman. Do not take a taxi, they pretend to be very helpful but in the end only want to make money. You should have a kind of passion for old inka places. Otherwise 70 PES / 18 € are not worth the entrance fee.

text_clean: The fountains are very nice and it could be a relaxing and enjoyable park. The park staff and police are trying to get a handle on the informal(illegal) venders in the park. Which for the most part are not that big of a deal, but when you are watching the fountain and light show and there is some guy repeatedly yelling out whatever he is selling and throwing things around to'advertise' it gets old real quick. All the police and park staff do is stand there with them. They don't actually remove them.

text_clean: Stopping locally so went in the morning (great time to go, as I was leaving at 1400 lots of minibuses had arrived and it was getting busy) I had the templo del sol all to myself for 15 minutes. Fantastic place with breathtaking views. After that I found half of it was closed off, maybe even more than half. Not a problem, if I'd been told when I came in and the main route map was still showing two routes you can't do. The place is fantastic, what I could walk round, and well worth a visit, but I'd be lying if I wasn't disappointed about the more than half I didn't know you couldn't walk round. Extra star back on for the water temple area to the right when you get in. This place is so well presented and relaxing I used the spare time I had from not being able to see most of the site to sit, relax, take in the view and read my book. A good visit but a little disappointing.

text_clean: Rating based only on the hike from Cabanaconde down to the oasis and back. The walk is doable in a day but made unpleasant by a deteriorating path and aggressive horsemen leading pack animals among it. Start early as there is no shelter between top and bottom. The oasis was clearly once beautiful, but is now marred by current and failed development attempts. Rubbish disposal appears to be a real issue. For the 70 soles entry fee I would hope better care can be taken of this area going forward.

text_clean: 1) YOU CANNOT WALK BACK, OR REPEAT ANY PART OF YOUR CIRCUIT. There are staff watching and they will blow their whistle and shout at you if you try. We arrived in a downpour so we walked to some shelter and waited it out. When we tried to walk back on the circuit we had paid for, we were not allowed. It doesn't matter that it's not busy, you want to take photos or any other reason, they will not let you walk back, even 20m. Unfortunately this was not clear when we purchased the ticket so we missed a significant portion of our route :( Therefore I'd advise you to walk slowly and enjoy the views, because once you arrive at the exit, that will be the end of your time at macchu pichu. I feel the staff lack compassion since it was 7am and quiet at macchu pichu, and I didn't want it to rain. If they had a cafe inside at the beginning I'd have waited there, but you have to enter at your time slot so I was left in a downpour in my poncho. I'd paid for flights, accomodation, and so many buses and trains to be there. The staff didn't show any compassion for my situation and were curt. 2) TOILETS WILL COST YOU EXTRA ON TOP OF THE ENTRANCE FEE, AND BAGGAGE STORAGE. Which I also find ridiculous considering the entrance ticket is so expensive. Toilet S/2 and baggage is S/5. 3) YOU CANNOT ENTER BEFORE THE ENTRANCE TIME ON YOUR TICKET Something to do with the system they use. 4) DO NOT FLY A DRONE The staff have a walkie talkie system and respond very quickly. I don't know what the consequences are, but the people we saw were taken to the office. 5) DO NOT BOOK EARLY MORNING TIME SLOT IF YOU ARE VISITING IN WET/ SHOULDER SEASONS The clouds tend to hang low in the sky in the morning so you might not have the best view/ you might have to wait for clouds to clear. 6) YOU CAN BUY THE BUS TICKET FOR THE BUS UPWARDS ONLINE, BEFOREHAND (12 USD per ride) Just look for the one bus company that runs the route. I can't remember off the top of my head. You can also purchase them at the bottom and at the top, the process is quick and easy. Hope this helps :) I did route 1&2 and was happy with all the areas it covered.

text_clean: Espectacular sitio de conexión entre la naturaleza y las familias que visitan el lugar. Desde el estacionamiento con 780msnm Inicia el ascenso por un camino adaptado y compatible con el entorno por un espacio de 15 min aprox, pasando por 2 puentes de madera sobre la misma quebrada del Ahuashiyacu. Llegas al lugar donde se encuentra la poza bajo la caída del agua con 900 msnm aprox. Ideal para estar con la familia y amigos, el lugar tiene clima templado de 18-22 Centígrados. Lo que hace muy agradable la estancia. Asimismo la vista y el espectáculo del agua (formas y sonidos) conectan La Paz del medio. Recomiendo visita * * * * * 👍🏻

The ruins look great and impressive but dishonest price gouging is simply ugly. We decided not to enter and walk around instead. That's 80% of the experience right there. Charge less or be clear at the entry to the climb that there is a charge so that people can make an informed decision beforehand.

That said, still a 5* experience. :) To say they are out in the sticks too would be a vast understatement!!!

text_clean: A big archaeological site from Inca time 20 min walk from the centre of Cusco. The building blocks are big and can weight a few tons. Not many is left of this site but it's nice to walk around. You need an extra bolleta touristica ticket to get in and it's not that cheap. If you have this ticket I would do it. If not ... There are places that are more interesting to visit.

text_clean: One of the historical Inca sites very close or even In Cusco. For me it was not exceptional. Just a lot of old foundations of buildings. However the view on Cusco city exceptional. I recommend visitng the site with the guide. If there is No one to talk even a little bit about it, it seems to be just a bunch of rocks. The entrance is 70 soles (or 130 soles for combo tickets for many sites and museums) and we paid 50 soles to the guide.

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: 1. Las imágenes se quedan cortas al compararlas con la vista en vivo y en directo. 2. Solo hay acceso a la huaca de la luna, ya que la huaca del sol aún no ha sido explorada (JUN22). 3. El servicio de guiado es gratis, sin embargo se recomienda hacer una colaboración voluntaria, ya que por pandemia estuvieron sin trabajo y el único ingreso que tienen los guías a mi parecer son las colaboraciones. 4. Al finalizar el recorrido hay una mini feria de artesanía, el precio es aceptable. Si llevas varias cosas te obsequian algo pequeño. No recomiendo regatear precios ya que son artesanos y viven de su trabajo. 5. Antes de comenzar el tour hay una cafetería, los precios algo altos pero te salvan de cualquier antojo. 6. La entrada se compra adelante, justo donde se encuentra el museo. Lo recomendable es comenzar el recorrido en el museo y luego ir por la huaca. 7. Recorrido aproximado de 1 hora. 8. El museo y la huaca cuentan con estacionamiento gratuito. A veces no hay guardián pero no se ve peligroso.
label: neutro

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Las líneas de Nazca, todavía no se sabe como han llegado allí, pero se trata de numerosos dibujos, algunos de más de 100m, hechos con perfectas líneas paralelas que se pueden ver desde el aire. text_clean: To be honest, before we got to Machu Picchu, I thought of it as a tourist trap of sorts, but that was very presumptuous of me. In reality, it is an AMAZING place, full of history and culture. It is beautiful and majestic, and puts one in awe of the genius of the ancient people, who had no writing, no benefit of modern tools, but were able to create such impressive structures that withstood time and nature. It is a very spiritual place, surrounded by incredible views of the mountains and the forest (and a bit of a tourist trap :) Definitely, you MUST have a knowledgeable guide who can tell you about the traditions of the ancient Incas, explain the architecture and provide a proper background story. We were very lucky to have such a guide. His name is Jimmy. He was very professional, was able to answer all of our questions and was great at taking pictures – he knew all the best spots and angles. He conducted his tour in English and demonstrated deep knowledge of the historical significance of the Machu Picchu site and the surrounding area. We took the bus up from the Machu Picchu Village to the archeological site and back. There is a pedestrian trail that one can take, they estimate about 2 hours up and about an hour down. It may be a good idea, but keep in mind that if the sun is out, it gets pretty hot going up the mountain. The site is divided into several Circuits. We bought tickets for the 2B Circuit, which allowed us to go up to the Guardian House and enjoy the views along the way, and then, access to the village, the king’s palace and the Temple of the Sun. This route offered magnificent views of the mountains and the Machu Picchu site, as well as a close up look at the buildings and passages within the site. I highly recommend this particular route. Our trip to Peru was a last minute, spontaneous decision, so we did not pre book our tickets to the Machu Picchu site. There was no way to purchase advance tickets, as they stopped being available about three months in advance. So, we decided to try our luck and proceed to the site hoping to get tickets there. Each day the Machu Picchu administration releases 1,000 tickets to Machu Picchu that can be purchase in person at the ticket office in Machu Picchu Village. A few days before we got there, they changed the way they process the ticket sales, which works out much better for those who wish to buy the tickets on site. That is, once ALL the 1,000 tickets are sold for the next day, they release 1,000 tickets for the following day, and so on. This is what we did: we arrived in Ollantaytambo from Cusco by a private car, and spent a night there. Then we took a train to the Machu Picchu Village and arrived there around 2 pm. We immediately went to the ticket office. By that time, they were selling tickets for the day after. We were able to book 2B Circuit, which is considered the best route through the archeological site. Once we had our tickets, we booked our return train to Ollantaytambo. So we ended up spending two nights in Machu Picchu Village, had our tour at 11 am and left on the train the same day, later in the afternoon. Please be advised that the train tickets you purchase from Ollantaytambo to the Machu Picchu Village (expensive as they are) ARE NOT the site entrance tickets. Those MUST be purchased separately. We witnessed a few people at the ticket office realizing that they booked all their train rides, but in fact, they did not have the site entry tickets and had to re-book their return train, because there were no Machu Picchu tickets available for the day they planned. Make sure you wear layers, because it may get pretty hot up at the Machu Picchu site. I would recommend a hat and insist on sunscreen. Finally, I can’t stress enough how important it is to have a competent guide while visiting the archeological site, it makes the experience complete.

We bought tickets for the 2B Circuit, which allowed us to go up to the Guardian House and enjoy the views along the way, and then, access to the village, the king’s palace and the Temple of the Sun. This route offered magnificent views of the mountains and the Machu Picchu site, as well as a close up look at the buildings and passages within the site. I highly recommend this particular route. Our trip to Peru was a last minute, spontaneous decision, so we did not pre book our tickets to the Machu Picchu site. There was no way to purchase advance tickets, as they stopped being available about three months in advance. So, we decided to try our luck and proceed to the site hoping to get tickets there. Each day the Machu Picchu administration releases 1,000 tickets to Machu Picchu that can be purchase in person at the ticket office in Machu Picchu Village. A few days before we got there, they changed the way they process the ticket sales, which works out much better for those who wish to buy the tickets on site. That is, once ALL the 1,000 tickets are sold for the next day, they release 1,000 tickets for the following day, and so on. This is what we did: we arrived in Ollantaytambo from Cusco by a private car, and spent a night there. Then we took a train to the Machu Picchu Village and arrived there around 2 pm. We immediately went to the ticket office. By that time, they were selling tickets for the day after. We were able to book 2B Circuit, which is considered the best route through the archeological site. Once we had our tickets, we booked our return train to Ollantaytambo. So we ended up spending two nights in Machu Picchu Village, had our tour at 11 am and left on the train the same day, later in the afternoon. Please be advised that the train tickets you purchase from Ollantaytambo to the Machu Picchu Village (expensive as they are) ARE NOT the site entrance tickets. Those MUST be purchased separately. We witnessed a few people at the ticket office realizing that they booked all their train rides, but in fact, they did not have the site entry tickets and had to re-book their return train, because there were no Machu Picchu tickets available for the day they planned. Make sure you wear layers, because it may get pretty hot up at the Machu Picchu site. I would recommend a hat and insist on sunscreen. Finally, I can’t stress enough how important it is to have a competent guide while visiting the archeological site, it makes the experience complete.
label: positivo

text_clean: Amazing place, this MAGIC CIRCUT OF WATER is a place where everyone can come and enjoy the beautiful flora, fauna and water on a daily evening show. This is the #1 icon of the city of LIMA. It has 13 gigantic water 💧 cybernetic fountains mixed with high technology where MUSIC, SOUNDS & LASER lights are mixed together to present a show that is unique to the Peruvian people and the world 🌎😀. We experienced an exciting and unforgettable show that was full of Colors, Music, Light and Magic. We were Very much enchanted and surprised by this great cybernetic show. This show is for all ages. We are so happy that our body guard suggested this attraction, thank you my friend and thank you to all the people from LIMA, PERU 🇵🇪😀 that have taken the time to talk to us and show us the great beauty of their country. Thank you GOD for giving us this amazing opportunity 🙏, AMEN. SINCERELY, TONY MORA 🙏❤💙♥.

text_clean: Para ir primero, toman carro en la terminal hacia kuelap que está 7 soles. Luego toman el teleferico que está 20,40 soles por persona que incluye ida y vuelta en la couster y teleférico 🚡 y que solo lo pueden usar hasta la hora que te indican, sino deberás volver a pagar los 20,40 soles para regresar. Al llegar deben pagar 30 soles por persona adulta para ingresar a kuelap, y finalmente subir a kuelap que es 30 minutos de caminata hacia arriba. De bajada es más rápido. A mi opinión personal, pienso que está sobrevalorado el costo de ingresar, ya que hay miradores con árboles de eucalipto y frutos en el camino, donde la entrada es 3 soles y que están cerca, asimismo podrían ir a las cataratas de gocta más temprano o los miradores de huancas y uno más que no me acuerdo. La verdad, que más me emocionó subir al teleferico 🚡 que llegar a kuelap, que fue la única decepción de mi viaje, además que ya no permiten ingresar a las casitas para tomar foto. Antes se podía según lo que me contó una persona que ya visitaba el lugar por segunda vez. Olvidaba, para ir a kuelap NO TOMEN GUIADO NI TOUR. no es necesario, es fácil de llegar.

text_clean: Es un lugar hermoso y de visita obligada como turista. Queda a 30 minutos de la ciudad, en la cordillera La Escalera. La entrada es de 6 soles. Para acceder no hay opción para ir con silla de ruedas, el camino es de 15 minutos de subida de escaleras y es más seguro si se coge de la baranda. Tiene dos niveles, donde hay y no hay piso. Recomiendo entrar con zapatillas de baño. El agua es helada, pero refrescante. Hay servicios higiénicos y lugares para cambiarse. En la catarata los guías dan recomendaciones con megáfonos, para estar protegidos (gracias por hacerlo).

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: If you appeared to be in Nasca (like us) and did not want to miss out on #1 tourist attraction here, think twice. We took the plane flight (85 eur/person) as thought it will be a unique experience. It’s still nice, BUT keep in mind: 1. The lines are smaller & less cooler than you think they are 2. The flight itself makes most people nauseous. Be ready for that, even if you did flights before. I got sick and numb in my arms, so even though I desperately tried, I could not enjoy it. To sum up: if you came here specifically to visit the lines, I guess you will like it, go for it. If you’re a casual tourist explorer like us, probably also ok to skip and do something else. Hope it helps to those who have a strong fear of missing out like me! :)

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: El museo está cerrado y los restos reales de la tumba de Sipán no se pueden visitar. Según el personal, hay problemas con el aire acondicionado y tardarán un año en volver a abrir completamente al público. Se pueden visitar tres espacios con réplicas y algunos hallazgos interesantes, pero si vienes a Lambayeque a ver al “Tutankamon peruano” te irás de aquí sin verlo. Es una lástima que el Ministerio de Cultura haya dejado un espacio como este en condiciones tan precarias y que no facilite la información con antelación. Me voy decepcionado y esperando que en el futuro se tomen en serio la protección y muestra de la rica cultura arqueológica de Lambayeque.

text_clean: What a waste of time! You go there and get tickets without a wait. The guide at the entrance who ended up being our guide during the tour told us the tour is mandatory and we have to tip her at the end. She repeated the tip part twice more during our tour but she was definitely bored and only interested in the money cause she would give us literally 1minute of basic info and then tell us to take photos while she walked on checking her phone for the entire hour! The info she gave us were marginally more than the info you read at the little room next to the ticket office and there was no explanation or contextualisation. Anyways, I felt it was a waste of time (the tour lasts one hour) and a shame she did not help us understand and appreciate more the ancient Lima culture. I was left confused because others have written amazing reviews so maybe it was just this lady guide that was really bad.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Es difícil puntuar como corresponde al Museo porque hay varios factores para tener en cuenta. Para empezar, cuando una entra al estacionamiento, tiene que volver a la entrada para adquirir el ingreso y el valor nos pareció más bien de índole simbólica * 10 soles para adultes * 4 soles para adultes mayores y estudiantes *1.50 soles niñes. Ingresamos en subida al mismo, y me quedé pensando, si la subida es tan empinada, ¿Cómo hacen para ingresar las personas con movilidad reducida o usuaries de silla de ruedas? Adentro del museo, el calor, hacia mitad del recorrido se hace realmente muy pesado, sumándole a la experiencia el uso obligatorio que tenemos que hacer como corresponde de la mascarilla. La exposición entera, está muy bien armada, sólo a veces (muy pocas en realidad) el relato construido para guiar la visita cambia de persona, al principio se mantiene con una "objetividad" y luego pasa a una primera persona colectiva. A tener en cuenta algunos de los termómetros no funcionan (: Es la cuarta vez que viajo hasta Lambayeque para poder compartir esta belleza de museo, en esta ocasión, fui acompañada de tres personas más y resulta que la última sala que es realmente la frutilla en el postre. Casi que el impulso principal para ver tan increíble sala viva, resulta que está inhabilitada, cerrada por covid. Lo cual, es ilógico debido a que estamos dentro del museo y esa sala, no se diferencia en exposición del resto de las salas. Así que, sigue siendo recomendable, pero deberían reactivarla.

text_clean: This is a great place to visit, I would say it is a must if you are visiting Lima. And if you are a resident in the Peruvian capital, it is a must visit every so often. The Park where the Magic Water Circuit is located is huge (8 hectares) and opens from 3:00 p.m. to 10:30 p.m. It is not only a park for the enjoyment of children, but for the whole family. It is very well preserved, located very centrally, and the prices (tickets) are very accessible: PEN 4.00 per person. It is a circuit of 13 computer-controlled fountains, which offer a fun, interesting and unique show of music, color, images ... and water. Do not miss it. text_clean: For me, it's an Overrated marketing success of an abandoned settlement. I will submit that the majestic mountains and valleys are the main draw and attraction. The hyped settlement architecture? It is just a cluster of abandoned stone/rock/mud low 'buildings' meant for 3-400 families. Sure, some walls are tightly stacked with big chipped/carved boulders/rocks and even a blade can't get thru the gaps... The mountain terrace carved/landscaped for agriculture is not unique to the Inca. The use of gravity to channel water is common amongst other old civilisations too... With a good guide, the story telling of Inca civilisation and the Inca connection to mother earth and nature can be engaging. Know too that getting there to MP is a long journey and time wasting pain, compounded with an eventual convoluted Q system to get expensive tickets. (It can be a 2 day Q for a 3hrs just one section visit of MP). .. And a tourist trap village venue to take a bus up to MP. When there, Also beware the nasty midges/sand flies which can carry the deadly oropouch virus. Only this is free. 🤣😂 Oh, the story of the hacked down obelisk to accommodate the Prez helicopter landing is outlandish.
label: negativo

text_clean: El cañon del colca tiene un paisaje imponente e inmenso, mucha flora y fauna. Hay que ir con buen calzado y un nivel intermedio de estado físico ya que hay que hacer trekking en altura y resulta medio dificultoso si no estás acostumbrado. Es viaje es bastante extenso, más o menos son 2 o 3 horas para llegar. No tuve la suerte de ver ningún Cóndor pero fue una linda experiencia. Esta excursión no la recomiendo hacer si vas a Perú con los días contados, hay lugares mejores.
label: positivo

text_clean: Not worth the time. Feels like a tourist trap replica of what might have been the discovery of a lifetime in the center of Lima. I had much anticipated visiting this sight, only to be met with a forced on guided tour with the world’s worst tour guide. I arrived having not much knowledge of the place and fully expecting to learn at least something how/what/when/why there were ruins in the middle of a metropolis such a Lima, but instead left what was to be an 1 hr guided tour approximately 20 mins into it. Later on, I read some of the Google reviews and it seems I must have had the same tour guide as many of them. She was boring, uninterested and has no place in being a tour guide. I say skip this place as it is currently ran. It’s a shame as I’m sure there is a very interesting fact as to how Huaca Pucllana came to be in the middle of Lima.

text_clean: Te va a gustar el sitio arqueológico si haces treaking o te apasiona la culturas peruanas, ya que el recorrido es bastante extenso y agotador. Hay algunas subidas muy elevadas. La vista frente al mar es bellísima. Por otro lado, el museo también es bellísimo, hay bastantes esculturas y herramientas pre incas. SIN EMBARGO, el trato de los guardias y guías que están en el museo de hostigante y ofensivo, puesto que te persiguen si es que vas por tu cuenta mirando y sin una guía, una de ellas me dijo “señorita por favor” y yo “??” Cuando solo estaba mirando sola una parte del museo. Además deberían mejorar el sistema ya que algunas compañeras no las dejaron pasar al museo porque perdieron su ticket. Amigos, ya estamos 2024, hagan un código qr o algo. Eso y que no te dejan entrar con tu mochila ni beber adentro agua. F

text_clean: Beautiful place and I would give it 10 stars. The views are breathtaking, the site is really big and worth seeing. The only thing I didn’t like is that one of the security guys was throwing rocks at lamas and dogs. I understand that they cannot go everywhere and they might destroy something, but why to throw the rocks directly at them and hurt them? I would be more glad (well, I would not, but let it be) if he threw the rocks near the animals to scare them, not aiming directly at them (and he was successful in aiming). The scared lama was running really fast and nearly ran at me. I reported it to the only guy that spoke English at the gate, firstly he laughed a little bit, but he told something to the security guy. Hope it changed something.
label: positivo

text_clean: If you have been in Tikal (Mayans culture - Guatemala) you not gonna feel impressive by Machu Picchu. Also it is really expensive all. Train, bus, entrance. Advices: #1 If you are with your own transport go to Hidroeletrica Machu Picchu, then park your vehicle there and go walk to Aguas Calientes (the closest city to the entrance of Machu Picchu site). You gonna walk around 2 hours but it gonna worth, once is all straight line, and train tickets are so expensive. #2 if you are on tour, you can take a van from Cusco or Urubamba (or even other cities) to Hidroeletrica Machu Picchu, and from there, follow the instructions on tip #1, but take a train from Cusco or wherever it gonna cost you 2837474 months worked. #3 There is no sale tickets at the entrance of the park. Yes, sounds weird. You have option to buy online, with a previous booking or at Aguas Calientes Cultural Centre (in Aguas Calientes). #4 Keep in mind there are 2 entrance open time: 6am and 12pm. But if you buy the tickets today for tomorrow or even for 3 days later may you not find the first period tickets. Once everyone has preference for morning time and wanna watch the sunrise. #5 there is no way to get at Machu Picchu by your own transport. #6 from aguas Calientes to the entrance of the site you gonna climb for 1h. But it worth the view are amazing, otherwise you can sell your kidney and go by bus, $12 going up, $12 going down (consider that 12 fu**ing dollars is lot of money in Peru). I guess that's all... feel free to contact me through here if you need more info about it. :) Ps.: no, I'm not working with tour. I'm just a traveler who wants enjoy the life pay what is fear and worth
